text: Global non-responders to exercise are unlikely to exist; apparent non-response is typically modality-specific rather than a universal failure to adapt to all forms of exercise.

Measurement error and the type of assessment used can falsely identify individuals as exercise non-responders, meaning some apparent non-response is an artifact of testing methodology rather than true biological non-response.
Increasing exercise intensity, volume, or duration can overcome apparent non-responsiveness in individuals who did not respond to an initial training intervention.

Exercise non-response appears to be modality-specific, meaning an individual who does not respond to one type of exercise may still respond positively to a different exercise modality or prescription.

Individually-adaptive approaches to exercise prescription — adjusting modality, intensity, volume, or duration based on individual response — are more effective than standardized programs for ensuring positive training adaptations across a population.
